Bring everything inside that could be a projectile.
Fill up your cars with gas and extra gas cans as well for generators etc.
Gather all important documents and place is waterproof protectant and keep with you.
Have cash on hand.
Board up windows if possible.
Get sandbags in place (stack like bricks) and for extra protection stack on top of tarp blocking entry points but make sure you have an escape route if needed.
Have suitcases packed in case of evacuation.
Have medications on hand.
Charge everything you can as far as portable chargers/lanterns/ipad/phones etc.
Know where your hurricane supplies are and get them ready. (Hand crank radio, lanterns, candles, tarps, rope, first aid kit etc)
Unplug all outlets so you don’t fry your electronics from power surges.
Make sure breaker is off when running generator and it’s outside not in your garage and away from your house.
Have food/snacks/bottled water readily available (don’t forget the hand can opener)
Fill sinks and tubs with water for flushing.
Fill old water bottles with water and keep next to sink for hand washing.
Fill Tupperware with water and put in freezer it will help keep your food cold if an outage.
Video and document your property and belongings in case of loss.
Have leashes and crates ready for your pets including records.
Pack food and bowls for them as well. NEVER LEAVE THEM BEHIND.
Safest place is inner most room away from windows.
If you’re in a flood zone and didn’t evacuate stay high and have your kayaks and paddle boards accessible juuuuust in case.

Great list but I would add a couple of things. Make sure you have some chargers that have a solar panel on one side so you can recharge them. Small evaporative coolers are great to keep you cool and most of them can run on small chargers for about 6 hours. We have several of them. Not that expensive and available at many places like Walmart, Target. Amazon etc. Every year we pack a "go bag" at the beginning of hurricane season and just leave it packed until the season ends...clothes, shoes etc. If you evacuate you just have to add a few extras like meds. I recently saw an idea that sounds feasible but we haven't tried it. ..thoroughly clean your washing machine and fill it completely with ice. Add well packaged food, milk etc and close the lid. Ice will last longer and the water drains out as the ice melts.

Hi, from a veterinary professional, please add the following for pet owners!
* stock up on needed pet medicine and food. Be aware your local vet clinic will be swamped with similar requests so DO NOT wait til the last minute! ESPECIALLY be sure to fill up on fear-free / anti anxiety meds if your pet needs them!
* make sure your pets' medical records INCLUDING RABIES TAGS are in your "go bag" in case you have to evacuate. If you are assigned to a temporary shelter such as a high school or civic center, many such places *will not let any animal in* if the owner can't provide proof of rabies vaccination on the spot. Your vet clinic will be closed by then so a phone call won't be able to clear anything up. Have DOCUMENTATION on hand!!
* get your cat carrier down out of the attic and clean it NOW. Get your dog's leash and harness ready and check them for fraying and appropriate fit. If you are in an emergency shelter, you will be expected to keep your animals appropriately controlled/ contained. You may be told they cannot be in the shelter with you any more if your animals are making other evacuees feel unsafe. It's not wrong or shameful to own and use a muzzle for your dog if they need it around strangers and scary situations. ❤
* if your pet has never been microchipped, consider having a microchip placed. If they are chipped, reach out to the chip monitoring company to make sure they have your up to date contact information. As a former shelter worker in FL, after every hurricane we would get an influx of lost pets. Many were unchipped or unregistered. We would hold for awhile in case an owner came looking - then up for adoption they would go! If your pet is separated from you in a storm, a microchip is BY FAR the best hope of your getting them back. If you know your pet's chip# but aren't sure if they're registered, here is a tool I use as a vet tech to look it up. It will tell you if the number is registered anywhere, then you can just reach out to the registry company to get your info up to date. (They will charge you a membership fee.)
Also once the water freezes put in a cooler if have one to reduce the temp as the water jug thaw switch it for a frozen one. Keep switching as long as you possibly can.
Keep some cash on hand in case internet goes down and cards are not usable… We found those out in WNC last week.
